entries.description: Loopt (2005–2012): Senior employees twice petitioned the board to remove Altman as CEO, citing issues with transparency and exaggerating company progress; consistent pattern of blurring the line between plans and accomplishments. Loopt ultimately failed to gain traction and sold for $43.4M in 2012.
entries.causation_evidence: Wikipedia (Sam Altman); The New Yorker investigation (Farrow/Marantz, April 2026); Analytics Insight timeline

entries.description: Y Combinator departure (2019): Multiple YC partners alleged Altman 'constantly lied' and prioritized personal interests over duties as YC president. Co-founder Paul Graham acknowledged a breakdown of trust. Ex-OpenAI board member Helen Toner stated the true reasons were 'hushed up at the time.' Conflict-of-interest concerns arose from Altman holding an indirect stake in OpenAI while simultaneously running YC.
entries.causation_evidence: The New Yorker (Farrow/Marantz, April 2026); TechCrunch; Helen Toner (TED AI Show podcast); Washington Post

entries.description: Microsoft investment clause deception (2019): When the $1B Microsoft investment closed, Altman inserted a clause giving Microsoft power to block OpenAI from mergers—directly contradicting OpenAI's founding safety commitments. When Dario Amodei (then OpenAI) confronted Altman about the clause, Altman denied it existed until shown the text. Amodei and colleagues later left to found Anthropic, citing 'the problem with OpenAI is Sam himself.'
entries.causation_evidence: The New Yorker investigation (April 2026, citing Dario Amodei's notes); Techloy summary of 8 New Yorker allegations

entries.description: Safety panel deception (December 2022): Altman told OpenAI board members that controversial GPT-4 features had been approved by a safety panel. Board member Helen Toner requested documentation and discovered the most controversial features—including user fine-tuning and personal assistant deployment—had never been approved. Altman also never disclosed to the board that Microsoft had released an early ChatGPT version in India without completing a required safety review.
entries.causation_evidence: The New Yorker investigation (Farrow/Marantz, April 2026); Techloy (8 allegations summary)

entries.description: OpenAI board firing (November 2023): Board unanimously ousted Altman, stating he was 'not consistently candid in his communications.' Chief Scientist Ilya Sutskever had compiled a ~70-page dossier of Slack messages, HR records, and analysis alleging 'Sam exhibits a consistent pattern of lying' and that he misrepresented safety protocols to the board. One board member described Altman as 'unconstrained by truth' and raised concerns about 'sociopathic' disregard for consequences of deception. An independent review was initiated but its conclusions were delivered only orally and did not fully endorse Altman's conduct.
entries.causation_evidence: Wikipedia (Removal of Sam Altman from OpenAI); The New Yorker (April 2026); Storyboard18; NewsNation; OpenAI board public statement

entries.description: Reinstatement power play (November 2023): After being fired, Altman set up a 'war room' at his San Francisco home, hired a crisis communications adviser, led an aggressive social media campaign, and leveraged aligned investors who conditioned funding on his reinstatement. Employees who hesitated to sign open letters demanding his return reportedly received pressure from colleagues. The entire board was subsequently restructured and made significantly less likely to challenge him. Critics noted this effectively dismantled the nonprofit governance safeguards.
entries.causation_evidence: The New Yorker (Farrow/Marantz, April 2026); Wikipedia (Removal of Sam Altman); Fortune

entries.description: Scarlett Johansson voice incident (May 2024): OpenAI launched a ChatGPT voice called 'Sky' that Johansson described as 'eerily similar' to her own. Altman had approached Johansson twice to license her voice; she declined both times. Altman proceeded to launch without her consent and posted 'her.' on X the same day, widely interpreted as acknowledging the intentional similarity. OpenAI paused Sky's use after public backlash. The incident followed the dissolution of OpenAI's Superalignment safety team and resignations of safety leads Sutskever and Jan Leike.
entries.causation_evidence: CNN Business; Axios; The Wrap; Scarlett Johansson public statement (May 2024)

entries.description: Safety culture degradation and NDA coercion (2024): Multiple senior safety and ethics leaders departed OpenAI citing safety being subordinated to product velocity—including Ilya Sutskever, Jan Leike (who said 'safety culture has taken a backseat to shiny products'), and policy researcher Gretchen Krueger. Separately, leaked exit agreements showed Altman's signature on documents imposing harsh NDA provisions threatening to claw back vested equity from departing employees who spoke publicly. Altman initially denied knowledge but documents showed his signature authorizing the provisions.
entries.causation_evidence: Time.com (Altman controversies timeline, June 2024); Fortune (OpenAI chaos, May 2024); CNN; Bloomberg (reviewed exit paperwork); Vox

entries.description: Annie Altman sexual abuse lawsuit (2025–ongoing): Sam Altman's sister Annie Altman filed a federal lawsuit in January 2025 alleging repeated sexual abuse and rape between 1997 and 2006 beginning when she was 3 years old. Sam Altman denied the allegations and filed a defamation countersuit. A first case was dismissed on statute-of-limitations grounds; Annie refiled an amended complaint under Missouri law in April 2026. The case is ongoing. Sam Altman is presumed innocent; allegations are unproven in court.
entries.causation_evidence: CNBC (January 2025); American Bazaar Online (April 2026); NewsNation; U.S. District Court Eastern District of Missouri filings

entries.description: Personal investment conflicts of interest (ongoing): Altman holds an extensive personal portfolio of 400+ companies valued at ~$2.8B including Helion Energy (nuclear fusion), Oklo (nuclear power), Reddit, Worldcoin, and Stoke Space. Altman pushed OpenAI to lead a ~$500M investment in Helion at a $35B valuation—more than 6x its prior round—directly benefiting himself. He also sought OpenAI collaboration with Stoke Space, in which his husband's family office holds a stake, without disclosing the relationship to all board members. Internal backlash intensified ahead of OpenAI's IPO at an $850B valuation.
entries.causation_evidence: Wall Street Journal (cited in BigGo Finance); Wikipedia (Sam Altman investments); OpenAI stepped down from Oklo chairmanship April 2025 'to avoid conflict of interest'

entries.description: Nonprofit-to-for-profit conversion (2024–2026): Altman oversaw OpenAI's conversion from a nonprofit-controlled entity to a public benefit corporation in which investors hold equity and voting control. The nonprofit's stake was diluted from full control to 26%. OpenAI's mission statement was changed six times since founding, with the 2025 IRS filing removing all references to 'safety.' Elon Musk's lawsuit (currently at trial as of April 2026) alleges Altman misled donors about the nonprofit structure. Critics including nonprofit governance scholars warn the changes explicitly prioritize profit over safety.
entries.causation_evidence: Fortune (February 2026, mission statement changes); Euronews (April 2026, Musk v. Altman trial); Fortune (March 2025, for-profit conversion); OpenAI IRS Form 990 (November 2025)

entries.description: New Yorker investigation (April 2026): Ronan Farrow and Andrew Marantz published a sweeping investigation based on 100+ interviews and internal documents portraying Altman as a 'pathological liar' with a 'consistent pattern of blurring the line between reality and speculation.' Sources across Loopt, YC, and OpenAI describe a career-long pattern of deliberate misrepresentation. One board member described him as having a 'sociopathic lack of concern for consequences of deception.' The investigation also found Altman used apocalyptic AGI rhetoric to reinforce his personal position while prioritizing personal gain. Hours after publication, OpenAI announced a new Safety Fellowship—widely seen as reactive PR.
entries.causation_evidence: The New Yorker (Farrow/Marantz, April 7, 2026); YNet News; Storyboard18; NewsNation; Techloy

entries.description: ChatGPT launch and AI democratization (2022–present): Altman oversaw the launch of ChatGPT on November 30, 2022, which became the fastest-growing consumer application in history and catalyzed a global AI revolution. By 2024, ChatGPT had grown to 300M+ weekly active users. OpenAI made its most capable models available free or at low cost without advertising. The platform has provided significant accessibility benefits including language preservation (Iceland), assistive tools for users with disabilities, and broad access to advanced reasoning tools.
entries.causation_evidence: Sam Altman blog (Reflections, January 2025); OpenAI Senate testimony (June 2023); TIME (December 2023); Analytics Vidhya

entries.description: AI governance advocacy (2023): Altman testified before the U.S. Senate in May 2023, voluntarily calling for AI regulation and government oversight—an unusual stance for a tech CEO at the peak of his company's growth. He advocated for safety-focused licensing requirements and international coordination on AGI risks.
entries.causation_evidence: OpenAI Senate testimony transcript (May 8, 2023); TIME CEO of Year (2023)
